text: I usually have something simple like toast with marmalade and tea. Maybe some yogurt if I'm feeling fancy. Sometimes my granddaughter brings me pastries or biscuits from her baking, so those are lovely too! Nothing too much fuss, really. A gentle start to the day is all I need.

I usually make myself a simple sandwich, perhaps some soup or leftovers from last night's dinner, and have it at my kitchen table while catching up on the news or chatting with the grandkids via video call. If I'm out running an errand, I might pop into the café nearby and grab something there – maybe a slice of cake or a salad. When my son comes to visit, we often go somewhere together; he knows me too well now so usually joins me for a walk after lunch anyway!

I tend to be an early riser, even though my body might protest a bit more now that I'm older! Normally, I aim for around 10 pm, give or take an hour, depending on how tiring the day has been. My grandkids like having me available in the evenings, so we'll have some lovely chats before bedtime, which helps me wind down a bit too.

I think a big part of my approach to self-care is being mindful of my physical limitations now that I'm older. So, while I still try to be active, like going for short walks each day, I make sure I listen to my body and take rest days when needed. For example, some afternoons might find me curled up with a good book or watching TV – nothing too taxing! Another aspect is staying hydrated and eating healthily, although I must admit I have been known to indulge every so often! A warm cuppa tea always helps start the morning, and a gentle walk around the block gets me moving. Occasionally, I'll treat myself to a nice bath with bubbles or an Epsom salt soak; those little luxuries feel wonderful. It's funny how simple pleasures become more cherished as we age, isn't it? Even getting enough sleep has become crucial – sometimes finding the motivation to wind down before bed takes effort, but I know it makes all the difference come morning. Taking time for hobbies is essential too – whether it's tidying the garden, chatting with the neighbour, or catching up with grandkids over Zoom. Those moments bring joy and connection, even if only briefly. Lastly, writing down memories, thoughts, and gratitude list really

I think networking is all about building genuine relationships with people around you, rather than just collecting business cards. In my former roles, I was always encouraged to be friendly and helpful, whether it was chatting with colleagues during tea breaks or attending local events related to our industry. Over time, those acquaintances turned into strong friendships and we'd support each other professionally whenever needed. When I joined a professional association, I made sure to introduce myself properly and ask others about their work – we still meet occasionally now. And after retiring, I started joining local groups focused on volunteering and hobbies which has helped me connect with like-minded individuals while giving something back to the community. It feels more organic that way; everyone's getting value out of these connections.

To be honest, I found working from home after being an office-based worker for so many years was a bit of an adjustment. But I think what helps me most is having some sort of structure, even though my work hours aren't traditional anymore since retiring. I try to create a dedicated workspace – which isn't always easy living in a two-bedroom flat! A nice desk with all my usual equipment, including my trusty writing notes and pens nearby. It makes everything feel more professional, you know? A daily walk outside during lunchtime really helps me recharge too; getting some fresh air does wonders for clearing one's mind. And I make sure to take breaks whenever I need them, whether it's to tidy around the kitchen or simply sit quietly with a cuppa for a minute or two. Even simple pleasures like that can refresh your focus. It's funny how much we used to rely on our colleagues and the bustle of an office environment to stimulate us... now I find myself missing those interactions. So, once or twice a week, I'll pop into the local café near mine for a coffee and catch up with fellow retirees who share similar schedules. Social connections still matter, even when we're not physically working together! Lastly, staying organised using digital tools and

I find video calls quite helpful, especially now that my grandkids live far away. It's lovely to be able to catch up with them face-to-face, even if we're apart physically. However, I must admit that sometimes finding the right equipment setup can be a bit tricky for me – I'm still getting used to using those tablets and smartphones, to be honest! But once everything is sorted out, it's wonderful being able to share moments and connect like normal. My grandson has been really helpful, teaching me how to navigate all these new-fangled gadgets...

Well, I think once you retire, your motivation comes from different sources, you know? You're still driven by a sense of purpose, perhaps more so since you're living life on your terms now. For me, staying motivated is about finding meaning in my daily routines. I like keeping busy, even though it might seem counterintuitive after decades spent being tied to an office job. I set little goals for myself – whether it's tidying out the attic, practicing piano, or spending time with grandkids. Having something to focus on gives me direction and helps me feel productive. Plus, having some flexibility allows me to take breaks whenever I need them, which is essential for me because...well, let's face it, getting older isn't always easy! It's all about balance, I suppose. And, I must admit, knowing I have plenty of free time has helped reduce any initial withdrawal symptoms from retirement. Now, what I really look forward to is seeing where each day takes me.

To be honest, I think most people struggle a bit with prioritising things, especially when there's so much going on! For me, it usually helps to take a step back, have a cuppa, and make a list of what needs sorting out. Sometimes breaking things down into smaller bits makes them feel more manageable, and focusing one thing at a time is better than getting bogged down in multiple issues all at once. In my earlier jobs, we had a system where each week was divided into sections – admin, correspondence, accounts... And really, it worked quite well until things got busy, which wasn't infrequent! Then it'd become chaotic, to be frank. Nowadays, since retiring, I find myself having to re-tune my approach because I'm not used to being bombarded every single day like I was before. Now, I set aside specific times for certain tasks and stick to those routines. It might sound simple, but finding some sort of rhythm has helped. I suppose what works best for me is making sure whatever I decide to tackle first gets done efficiently. If something urgent pops up later, I'll deal with it, but initially, focus on completing what's already been decided upon. Afterward, if needed, reassess and adjust

I think advocating for myself has become second nature after all these years of being a dedicated and hardworking employee. You know, back in my teaching assistant days, I'd speak up if something wasn't quite right – whether it was an issue with resources or concerns about pupil wellbeing. And later, when I worked in the admin side, I made sure to clarify any misunderstandings or discrepancies, ensuring everything ran smoothly. Nowadays, as a retiree, I still like to offer guidance where needed, though perhaps more informally. My grandkids appreciate my input on various projects, and we have lovely chats about what works best for them. When I'm out and about, be it running errands or chatting with neighbours, I express my opinions politely yet firmly, making sure others hear me loud and clear! It really is saying "no" occasionally; sometimes people forget how valuable your voice is! For instance, I'll say "No thank you" kindly but clearly if someone offers me food when I've already had enough. Or, I might decline social invitations because I need some downtime. These little actions show respect for myself and those around me, which helps prevent burn-out.

I think I take a deep breath and try to look at things from another perspective. You know, when we're all getting older like me now, our minds aren't what they used to be! Sometimes, people might misinterpret my words or tone because they haven't known me for ages. So, I give them the benefit of doubt, talk it out calmly, and ask clarifying questions to make sure we're on the same page. And usually, after having an open conversation, everything gets sorted out nicely. It's amazing how much better communication is when we listen properly and explain ourselves clearly.

I love being around my family! At our gatherings, we usually have a lovely time catching up and sharing stories. My granddaughter is always so full of energy and enthusiasm, and my son loves spoiling her rotten, which makes me smile every time. We like to take turns asking each other about what's been going on in everyone's lives – work, hobbies, any funny moments... You know how it is! We tend to stick to simple meals together, maybe some traditional Sunday roasts or homemade pasta dishes. I'm quite proud of my cooking, even if I say so myself! After lunch, we might play games, watch a film, or go for a walk in the nearby park. It's wonderful to spend quality time together and make memories. It's nice to be able to relax and unwind without too much fuss, knowing that everything will sort itself out afterwards. Sometimes, I'll bring along an old photo album or two to share some fond memories with them, and we all sit around reminiscing about the past. Those are always special moments for us.

I'm not really sure what all these platforms are like nowadays, but my son says there's a thing called LinkedIn where people showcase their work experience and connect with others. To be honest, I find it all a bit overwhelming. I mean, why bother sharing everything about yourself online, isn't that just an invitation for strangers to contact you? In my line of work, we were always quite private about our personal lives, so the idea of putting myself out there seems a bit too much for me. Still, some people seem to enjoy using it, and I suppose it might be useful if I wanted to look for part-time work or networking opportunities. But I think I'll stick to keeping in touch with acquaintances through emails and letters, thank you very much!

I think I'm quite honest, but I try to be tactful too. If someone is close to me, like a child or grandchild, I'll usually try to explain how I feel without being too graphic. But with people who aren't familiar with my situation, such as colleagues or acquaintances, I might be more guarded at first before opening up. It really depends on the individual and our relationship, I suppose. My late husband used to say I was very direct, which sometimes got us into trouble, but he always appreciated my candour. Now, since his passing, I find myself having fewer conversations where sensitivity isn't already paramount – thankfully!

Well, I'd say I'm quite stubborn once I make up my mind, so I find it rather difficult to be swayed one way or another unless there's a strong reason for changing course. As someone who's always valued being honest and direct, I believe in speaking my truth, even if it's unpopular. However, I try to consider others' feelings too – after all, relationships are more enjoyable that way! My husband used to tease me about never saying "yes" sometimes, but he was probably right; I have been known to hold out until someone convinced me otherwise! I think what helps is having a sense of confidence in oneself. When we know our own minds, it becomes easier to stand firm against external pressures. Of course, knowing exactly why something's wrong with an idea isn't essential either – a bit of common sense usually does the trick. But ultimately, staying true to myself has worked pretty well throughout the years.

To be honest, I'm a bit wary of those targeted ads. I mean, how do companies know what my likes and dislikes anyway? It seems like an invasion of privacy to me. My husband always said something similar when he was around – we didn't trust all these big corporations knowing so much about our personal lives. Now that he's gone, I find myself being even more cautious. If I really need to buy something, I'll check out reviews or ask someone else before making a purchase. But for everyday stuff, I try to avoid clicking on them altogether. Don't think it's worth risking having some stranger know where I am or what I might be interested in buying.

To be honest, I struggle a bit with managing multiple browser tabs. It was always quite simple enough when we first got these computers, but now everything seems so complicated! My grandson is always telling me to switch to a better system, something like Chrome with all its bells and whistles... But honestly, I find it overwhelming. Sometimes I'll have three or four open at once – my main website, an email, maybe a Wikipedia page for research, and sometimes I'll try to look up something while talking to someone else. I usually close them one by one after finishing what I needed, but some days I feel frustrated because there seem too many options popping up and asking me to download stuff or sign up for accounts. A bit too much information for my liking! Still, since my grandson helped set up my tablet, he says I should start using the taskbar feature which helps keep similar websites together, so perhaps that might make more sense soon.

Well, I'm a straight shooter, so if someone says something nasty or hurtful, I'll tell them how I feel, firmly but politely. You know, a bit like my husband used to say – "stick to your guns"! If necessary, I might block or limit contact with people who persistently cause trouble. But generally, I try to rise above all that nonsense. After all, there's enough kindness and goodwill around; we should focus on those rather than indulging negativity. My grandmother always said, "Don't let the crows bully you out of the tree." It stuck with me, and I think it's sound advice.

If there's a bit of a hitch with my video connection, I usually try to remain patient and speak slowly, hoping someone will pick me up again. If it continues, I might ask the other party to wait while I troubleshoot myself – I'm still getting used to these new-fangled devices! My grandchild is always happy to lend a hand; he knows how much I struggle with all the modern tech. Sometimes we'll even have a nice chat until our internet sorts itself out, making sure everything runs smoothly before we start talking properly.

I always try to stick to my budget and plan out my finances a week ahead of time. I make sure to set reminders on my calendar so I never miss any deadlines. And I'm quite particular about keeping track of everything – I have all our expenses listed out in a notebook, including receipts and invoices. It helps me keep an eye on where we're spending money and catch any discrepancies before they become major issues. Plus, having a bit of wiggle room in case of emergencies is essential to me. My husband used to say I was very methodical about these sorts of things, even back when he could still read between the lines! After he passed away, I found myself being more diligent than ever, knowing how much reliance I had on him financially after his passing. Now, I like to think it's because of habit rather than necessity, but perhaps there's some truth to saying I'd rather be safe than sorry!

I'm quite methodical about my subscription management, thank you for asking! Whenever a service comes up for renewal, I make myself sit down with a cuppa and review all the ones we have. If I haven't used something for ages or it's simply too pricey, out it goes! But before cancelling, I always think about whether there might be some hidden benefits I should know about – sometimes these things come free after a trial period, like streaming services. I'd rather pay upfront knowing exactly what I'll get, so cancellation isn't an option. And if someone has recommended it to me and I really value their opinion...well, I tend to give them another chance. Still, my motto is "useful, not unnecessary", as Grandma used to say!

I think before making a big ticket purchase, like replacing my current washing machine, I weigh all the pros and cons very carefully. It helps me consider whether it's something we really need now versus saving money for other important things, like setting aside some extra for our grandkids in case we might need to cover any future expenses related to them. And sometimes I'll even make a little list to tick boxes so everything gets considered properly. Old habits die hard! My late husband used to say, "a penny saved is a penny earned," and I still believe that today.

I'm a firm believer in being frugal, even after all these years of saving and living below my means. If something is still in decent condition and serves its purpose, there's little reason to spend more money on a brand-new one. My late husband always said I was like an onion – layers upon layers! But seriously, we never went into debt together, so our motto has been "buy once, buy wisely." Unless absolutely necessary, I'd much rather look for bargains and make the most of what I have. Plus, with me having had some experience with typewriters in my early days, I've grown accustomed to making do with older models; sometimes it takes getting used to them again!

I think my upbringing had a lot to do with it. My mum always taught me that waste is just an opportunity to save something better instead, so we never really went into debt even when I was growing up. It stuck with me throughout my working life too. When I started earning money, I made sure to set aside what little I could each month, whether it was saving for holidays or paying bills early. And whenever there were any tempting purchases, I'd ask myself "will I still need this after six months?" If the answer was yes, I'd wait until I got some more money before buying it. Even now, I'm very much mindful of spending – I take advantage of sales, try to shop around for prices, and I know exactly how much I have coming in every month, which helps me stick to my budgets. Just knowing that I've worked hard for all those years has given me a sense of security, and I feel like I've been able to achieve quite a bit while keeping out of debt.

I love a good sale! You know, my late husband always said I was like a magpie, picking up all sorts of bargains whenever there were discounts. Now, since he's gone, I try to stick to my usual habits. When we have sales at our local stores, I make sure to take advantage of them, but only what I really need, mind you. I'm not one for impulse buying. If something's essential, I'll grab it during the sale – think kitchenware, linens, maybe some clothes for the grandkids. But non-essentials... well, let me tell you, those go straight into the "maybe" pile. After a bit of deliberation, I usually decide whether it's worth saving the money or just buy it anyway. My motto is 'useful now' rather than 'cheap'.

Portion control is really something my mother taught me growing up. You know, during rationing after the war, we had limited food portions anyway! So, even though we didn't have all these fancy marketing techniques now, I think it stuck with me. Now, I'm quite mindful of what I eat and try to pace myself. It's not about depriving yourself, but being sensible about how much you're consuming. And I must say, having a bit of discipline around eating helps prevent overindulgence. I'd rather save room for dessert, wouldn't you agree?

I think a gentle and honest one, really. You know, my late husband always said I was very open with him, so I suppose I'll carry that on here too. If someone asks me something personal, I'd like them to share what's troubling them and we could have an easy chat about it. I'm not looking for some fancy jargon or quick fixes; I just need to understand why people feel certain way and work together to find solutions. Maybe that sounds old-fashioned, but I believe sometimes talking through our thoughts helps us sort ourselves out better. Plus, I'm sure there's nothing wrong with being a bit vulnerable – after all, isn't that how friendships and relationships begin?

Preventing burnout is a constant concern for me these days, given my history of managing demanding jobs like being an administrator. To be honest, after retiring, I try to stick to simple routines and make sure I have some flexibility built into my daily schedule. A bit of free time each week allows me to recharge – whether that's watching TV, taking a leisurely walk, or spending time with the grandkids. I'm quite particular about prioritising my tasks, too; focusing on one thing at a time helps avoid feeling overwhelmed. And of course, getting enough sleep isn't something I forget! Getting out and about regularly keeps me feeling more alert and energised overall, though I'll admit, bus travel can sometimes feel tiring due to all those transfers. I think what really helped me was having support during my working years, knowing someone could turn to when needed made everything much easier. Now, while living alone, staying close to loved ones and keeping them informed whenever necessary makes all the difference. It's funny how essential human connection is, even now we're older! Oh, and maintaining an interest outside of work has always been vital – for instance, I love gardening – gets me moving outdoors and helps me unwind. Simple pleasures, perhaps, but they give

To be honest, getting a good night's rest has become more challenging since my husband passed away six years ago. But I still try to establish a regular bedtime routine – around 10 pm is what works best for me now. A warm bath, maybe some gentle stretching, and then a nice cup of herbal tea before bed helps signal to my body that it's winding down. I find it essential to make sure my room is dark, quiet, and cool, so I use blackout curtains and earplugs when needed. My grandkids think it's funny how much I fuss about making sure everything is perfect for sleeping! It might seem excessive, but trust me, after years of taking care of others, it's comforting to have control over one aspect of my life like that. It's also crucial for me to avoid screens for an hour or two before bed; all those blue lights interfere with my circadian rhythms. I know I should update my smartphone and computer settings to reduce screen glow, but sometimes I'm too lazy... And speaking of which, I really need to ask my son to lend his e-reader instead. His wife bought him an excellent model specifically designed to minimize eye strain! Overall, consistency seems key. As long as I stick to my routine and create

I try to mix things up a bit to avoid boredom. Since my mobility isn't what it used to be, I tend to focus more on gentle exercises like chair yoga or seated stretches. My grandkids love coming over and we'll sometimes have dance parties together while watching music videos – it gets me moving even if it is just waltzing around the living room! In terms of actual physical activity, I usually stick to shorter sessions, maybe 10-20 minutes max, so I'm not exhausting myself. A brisk walk along our local park path or some light gardening helps keep me feeling relatively energetic. On special occasions, my son will take me out for a drive and we might visit one of those lovely countryside walks near here. It's wonderful getting out into nature. Simple pleasures, really! Occasionally, I treat myself to an indoor swimming session or join in on a water aerobics class once every now and then. Those classes remind me how much fun movement should be – all the laughter and camaraderie make the effort worthwhile! Nothing too strenuous though; must remember my body still has its limitations after decades of hard work.

I think I tend to weigh them out step by step, considering what each option entails and how it might impact my daily life. If there's a particular issue or decision I need to make, I'll usually start by listing all the pros and cons – even if it feels a bit tedious sometimes! Then, I try to consider who else will be affected by the choice and whether any alternatives have been overlooked. Sometimes, having someone like one of my grandkids talk me through an idea helps me see it more clearly, too. Ultimately, I'm looking for something that balances benefits against hassle, makes sense practically, and aligns with what's truly important to me.

I think incorporating feedback is crucial, really. When someone takes the trouble to give me something to consider, I always try to listen attentively and put myself in their shoes. It helps me to identify areas where I might have been wrong or could improve upon. Then, I take a moment to weigh my thoughts and feelings before responding or making any changes. Sometimes, it's helpful to step away and revisit the issue later with fresh eyes – having some space to gather my thoughts before reacting. It's funny, isn't it? As an admin assistant, I was always taught that constructive criticism was essential, so I'm rather used to taking it on board now. Of course, there are occasions when I may feel defensive or resistant to what others say; however, I remind myself that it's usually because I genuinely believe in my work and value my expertise. So, after all, giving and receiving feedback go hand-in-hand. What one expects will be taken seriously depends on trust, respect, and open-mindedness, which seems like quite a tall order! Still, we must strive for improvement, surely? After considering everyone else's input, I try to find common ground or middle-ground whenever feasible. In our team meetings, we discuss concerns openly and honestly to arrive at decisions

I think my approach is quite straightforward, really. When faced with a problem or situation, I try to break it down into its components, considering all the relevant factors one by one. It's like taking apart an engine, making sure each part is properly aligned before putting everything together again. Sometimes, you have to step back, look at the bigger picture, and consider different viewpoints before coming up with a solution. And, of course, being meticulous and paying close attention to detail helps too – there's nothing worse than overlooking something crucial! I suppose you could say I'm methodical in my approach, always striving to make sense of things rather than leaping to conclusions.

I find that writing things down helps me process my thoughts. If something is bothering me or I'm unsure about something, I'll jot down a note or make a list so I can work out what I think about it later. Sometimes talking to someone close like one of my grandkids will help too – they're always happy to listen! And I have to admit, having some quiet time to myself to reflect on things does wonders for clarifying my mind. It's amazing how much clearer everything becomes after taking a bit of time to think it through.

I think one way I'd identify areas for growth is noticing when something isn't quite going smoothly, like a task taking longer than expected or causing more hassle than necessary. Then, I try breaking it down into smaller steps, looking at what went wrong, and finding solutions before tackling it again. It might be helpful to have someone else take a look too – sometimes fresh eyes spot something I haven't thought of. Writing notes and checking them regularly helps me track progress and remember lessons learned. And really, it comes down to being honest with myself, acknowledging where I'm struggling and making conscious decisions to work on those weaknesses rather than letting them build up.

I think my approach is quite simple, really. I like getting into a situation before diving too far in, taking some time to consider all sides of things. Sometimes that might mean asking myself what others have done in similar circumstances, looking for lessons they could share. And once I feel fairly certain which way I'm inclined to go, I try breaking it down into manageable bits – smaller steps rather than feeling overwhelmed by the whole picture. Of course, there'll always be surprises along the way, but having an open mind helps me navigate those twists and turns better. As I said, "patience and curiosity" seem key to making progress.

I make sure to set aside some dedicated time each day, usually after my morning cuppa, to catch up on a newspaper or listen to a podcast while having lunch. It's lovely to unwind and stay informed about what's going on in the world. When I'm feeling peckish, I might have a look at cookbooks or recipe websites too – always looking for inspiration for meals I can share with the grandkids! Occasionally, our granddaughter will join me and we'll chat about her day or something interesting we've seen together. So, even though I may be retired now, I still try to fit in some relaxation and mental stimulation into my daily routine.

I find out about new authors or genres mostly through word-of-mouth recommendations from my grandkids or friends. They're always eager to share what they've read lately, and we have a lovely chat about books afterwards. Occasionally, I'll pick up a bestselling novel in a shop while running some errands, and sometimes I stumble upon an author's work in the local library. The woman who delivers our mail has recommended several authors to me too; she knows all sorts of interesting writers! Sometimes, my granddaughter introduced me to authors like Joanne Harris – her enthusiasm is infectious, isn't it? It really is amazing how much joy discovering new writing brings, even after so many decades since finishing secondary school. It warms my heart knowing there are still plenty more great tales waiting to be discovered!

I love putting on some gentle tunes while I'm getting ready in the morning or tidying around the house. It really helps me set a relaxed atmosphere and gets my mind going for the day ahead. Sometimes I'll put on an old favourite record from when I was younger, like The Beatles or Doris Day – those classic sounds always bring a smile to my face! Other times, I might listen to something more contemporary, maybe a bit of classical music or nature sounds – I find it all very soothing. But mostly, I suppose what I enjoy most is simply letting the music be in the background, allowing myself to think quietly before starting my daily routine.

I find them quite enjoyable, actually. When I'm doing something else around the house, like tidying up or cooking, I'll put on a podcast or listen to an audiobook. It's lovely having someone to chat with while still being able to focus on my daily tasks. I prefer longer programmes though, so I usually opt for full series rather than individual episodes. The BBC is always one of my go-to sources; I find their documentaries really informative and engaging. My grandkids think it's hilarious watching me getting engrossed in these sorts of shows!

I'm quite fond of taking daily short walks around my neighbourhood. The fresh air does me a world of good, even though some people might find them a bit too leisurely-paced nowadays! When the weather permits, I like to stroll along the nearby canal path – it's peaceful and gets me out of the house for a bit. Occasionally, one of my grandkids will come visit, so we'll take a walk together; those moments always put a big smile on my face. Sometimes, if there's a local event happening in the village, such as a farmers' market or a fair, I'll join in for a bit. Nothing too strenuous, mind you, but enough to keep myself occupied and feeling sociable.

I must admit, after having spent so many hours juggling work and family responsibilities throughout my life, I rather cherish a bit of solo time now that I'm retired. It's lovely being able to potter around the garden, tend to my plants, and simply sit quietly in my favourite armchair with a cuppa. Sometimes I'll read a book, watch an old film, or take a walk along our local canal – there's something peaceful about being surrounded by nature. Really, it's wonderful to have some time just to myself, where I can unwind and reflect without any distractions.

I'm always eager to try something different, even if it might be a bit out of my comfort zone. You know, after all those years of traditional British cooking, I like to think I'm quite adventurous now! When we go out to eat or have someone bring home an exotic dish, I'll usually start by asking them what ingredients were used so I can make sense of it. Then, I take tiny bites, savor each flavor, and ask lots of questions – "How does it taste?" "Is there any spice involved?" or "Can you explain how it's prepared?" If I really enjoy something, I love making notes and recording the recipe. My grandkids find it hilarious watching me scribble away, muttering to myself while tasting every little thing. Sometimes they tease me about being the most enthusiastic foodie grandma around! But honestly, I believe that exploring new flavors is part of living life fully; everyone should indulge in culinary adventures whenever they can. It's never too late to discover new favorites, isn't it?

I think it's essential to have a mix of activities that suit my pace and preferences. You know, I'm an ambivert, so I enjoy being around people, but I also need some time to myself to recharge. With the grandkids coming to visit regularly, we always make sure to plan something fun together – whether it's baking, going out for a walk, or playing board games. They're such a joy! When I'm feeling more energetic, I like to take advantage of our local area. We'll go into town, grab a cuppa, catch up with neighbours, or attend community events. It's lovely to feel connected to the community. Occasionally, I might join a gentle exercise class or attend a gardening workshop; these allow me to be outdoors while still keeping busy. As for other hobbies, I used to love painting, which I haven't done much since retirement. Maybe one day soon, though! For now, writing is another interest – jotting down memories and family history fascinates me. My son has even helped me start digitising those old photo albums, what a treasure trove! Overall, finding the perfect balance between activity and rest is key for me. Sometimes it's simply enjoying a lazy Sunday morning, watching the

I have a little notebook where I jot down my plans for the week ahead, including any outings I'm looking forward to, like going to visit the grandkids or taking a walk around the park. It helps me feel more organised and makes sure I don't forget anything important. My granddaughter has even been known to draw pictures in there too!

I'm a bit old-fashioned, but I still swear by my trusty notebook and pen! Whenever something comes into mind, whether it's a task list, reminders, or notes, I jot them down in a spiral-bound notebook with plenty of blank pages left over for future additions. It's been a habit since I was young, really – even during those secretarial courses at college, we were always encouraged to keep a logbook for proofreading exercises. For more substantial projects, like managing the surgeries where I used to work, I'd set up folders and labels on my desk and create index cards for each file. Then, there's my current system for keeping track of my daily routines and grandkids' schedules. My son helps me out with updating digital calendars on his phone, which is nice because he's tech-savvy enough to make sure everything stays synced across all our devices! At home, I have a simple filing cabinet with labeled drawers for bills, receipts, insurance documents... basically any paperwork that requires storage space. And let's not forget the old adage "a place for everything," so I try to put away items after using them straightaway. It might sound too much effort now, but trust me, it makes such a difference having a tidy

Before leaving home, I usually make myself a cup of tea, put my coat on, grab my handbag, and check the weather forecast so I know what clothes to wear. If it's going to be wet, I'll take an umbrella too! Sometimes, I might jot down any reminders on a piece of paper, like phone numbers or notes for me to pick up later, just in case I need them while out. And I always try to catch the early morning bus – nothing worse than running late because I overslept! It's such a simple start to the day, really.

I try to be quite mindful of my energy usage, dear. Since I'm getting older now, I think it's essential to reduce our carbon footprint a bit more. We have draught-proofing measures done around the place – like putting those draughty curtains up and having an extra layer of insulation. And we switched to LED bulbs ages ago; couldn't believe how much better they're on our bills! Also, since my husband passed away, I found myself using the heating a lot more because I was lonely sometimes. But now, whenever the weather gets chilly outside, I make sure to put the thermostat down a notch while still keeping warm enough indoors. It's all about finding that balance, isn't it? We also invested in some energy-efficient appliances, like a new boiler, which has been a big cost-saver. My son came round one winter morning and told me what I should consider installing solar panels; he reckons that'll really cut our fuel bills. Still haven't got there yet, but maybe soon...

To be honest, I'm still getting used to them all! My grandkids think I should really update my gadgets more frequently, but I like having control over what I have, so we compromise a bit. At present, I only have an Amazon Echo Dot which is easy enough for me to manage, mostly because one of my sons helps out sometimes. It does make things easier around the house though – I can ask it to remind me when medication time comes round or tell me the weather forecast. Still, I find some of these new-fangled contraptions can be quite overwhelming; too much information and fussiness for an older brain such as mine!

I think prioritising is all about weighing what's really important to me and focusing on those things first. For instance, my grandkids are always asking me to spend more time with them, so spending time together is definitely a priority for me. And staying relatively financially secure, too – making sure there's enough money set aside for any eventual care costs, like nursing homes or something like that. It's funny how sometimes people say 'use your golden years wisely', but honestly, I think being able to live comfortably isn't the only thing; having connections with loved ones and keeping an eye out for local issues where I can make a difference seems rather crucial, even though we're getting older. So, balancing everything and knowing exactly what has to be done every single week... It's hard work, but someone's got to do it! As Granny used to say, "If you want the rainbow, you have to put up with the rain." You know?
